# Delta — LLM developer tutorial (no SDK required)

> Delta is a single-binary multi-model database: documents + vectors +
> Redis-style cache + RBAC + replication. It exposes the **same logical API**
> over three transports on three ports:
>
> * `deltaql://<host>:16889` — **the recommended way for any non-trivial
>   client.** A persistent, stateful, multiplexed binary protocol — the
>   MySQL-equivalent for Delta. Connect once, log in once, then fire any
>   number of requests AND receive server-pushed pubsub messages on the
>   exact same socket forever. No per-request TCP/HTTP setup tax. No
>   re-authentication. ~2–6× the throughput of HTTP-per-request.
> * `ws://<host>:16890` — the same persistent protocol, but each frame is
>   a JSON text message. This is the deltaql:// variant browsers can speak.
> * `http://<host>:16888` — plain REST. Use this for one-off `curl`
>   commands, scripts that fire one or two requests, or anywhere that
>   can't keep a long-lived socket. Every request pays a TCP+HTTP setup
>   cost, and you have to attach the `Authorization` header to every call.
>
> **Rule of thumb:** if your agent will issue more than ~10 requests, or
> wants live pubsub, **open one `deltaql://` (or `ws://`) connection and
> reuse it for the whole session.** Don't open a new connection per
> request — that defeats the entire point.
>
> This file is the source of truth. It is fetchable from any running
> Delta server at `GET /llms.txt`, and from
> <https://raw.githubusercontent.com/ProtectsAPP/delta/main/llms.txt>.

---

## 1 · Pick your transport

| Use case                                               | Transport          | Pattern |
| ------------------------------------------------------ | ------------------ | ------- |
| Backend service, agent, daemon, anything long-lived    | `deltaql://:16889` | **One persistent connection per process** |
| Browser, mobile, edge runtime, anything with no raw TCP | `ws://:16890`     | One persistent WebSocket per page/session |
| One-off curl, migration script, ad-hoc shell snippet   | `http://:16888`    | Per-request HTTP (re-send `Authorization` each time) |

All three transports share the **same JSON shapes** for every endpoint —
filters, update operators, response envelopes are identical. The only
differences are framing and how you carry the auth token.

The response envelope is always:

```json
{ "code": 200, "data": { ... }, "message": "..." }
```

* `code` is the Delta `Status` enum, **not** the HTTP status:
  `OK=0 ERROR=1 NOT_FOUND=2 DUPLICATE=3 INVALID=4 UNAUTHORIZED=5 FORBIDDEN=6 INTERNAL=7`.
* On error, `data` is `null` and `message` carries the reason.
* List endpoints wrap items: `data.users`, `data.documents`, `data.items`,
  `data.members`, `data.collections`, `data.roles`. Always read the key,
  not just `data`.

Default credentials: `admin / admin` (change immediately in production).

---

## 2 · The persistent connection — `deltaql://`

This is **the** way to use Delta. It is a real persistent stateful TCP
connection — the direct equivalent of a MySQL connection — except:

* multi-request multiplexed (many requests in flight on one socket,
  correlated by a 32-bit `rid`) — MySQL can't do this
* server-push pubsub on the same socket — MySQL can't do this either
* one TCP+JSON handshake instead of MySQL's binary handshake +
  capability flags + per-statement prepare; same persistence guarantees

The server holds these per-connection (verified in `src/network/deltaql_server.hpp`):

| Per-connection state | What it does |
| --- | --- |
| `int fd_`              | the TCP socket; lives until you close it or send `BYE` |
| `std::string token_`   | sticky auth — set once on AUTH, auto-injected into every REQUEST |
| `std::set<std::string> subs_` | your channel subscriptions; PUBLISH frames are pushed to you whenever anyone publishes |
| reader thread          | parses incoming frames |
| writer thread          | serializes outgoing frames so concurrent responses don't interleave |

So: you do not need to install any SDK and you do not need to "manage a
connection pool" — you literally just open one socket per process and
keep it alive. The 80-line Python class in §2.2 below **is** your
client. Copy it into your project verbatim.

Pseudocode of the lifecycle:

```
open one TCP socket to host:16889       (once, at process start)
  → HELLO  { "client": "your-agent/1.0" }
  → AUTH   { "username": "admin", "password": "admin" }
       (server stores the token on this connection — sticky for life of socket)

for as long as your process runs:
  → REQUEST { method, path, body, ... }      ← every API call goes here
  ← RESPONSE { status, body }                  (correlated by `rid`)

  → SUB     { channel: "trades" }             ← optional: subscribe to pubsub
  ← PUBLISH { channel, message }              (server-push, any time)
  ← PUBLISH { channel, message }
  ...

→ BYE                                          (graceful shutdown)
close socket
```

Key properties:

* **Sticky auth.** AUTH happens **once**. The server attaches your token
  to the connection and auto-injects it into every subsequent REQUEST.
  You never re-send `Authorization`.
* **Multiplexed.** Every frame carries a 32-bit `rid`. You can have
  thousands of REQUESTs in flight at once and match responses by `rid` —
  no head-of-line blocking like HTTP/1.1.
* **Live pubsub on the same socket.** SUB once, then PUBLISH frames
  arrive whenever someone (anywhere) calls `POST /api/v1/pubsub/publish`.
  No polling, no second connection.
* **TCP_NODELAY + SO_KEEPALIVE** are set by the server. The connection
  stays alive across NAT idle timeouts as long as you send something
  every few minutes (a PING frame is enough).
* **Graceful close.** Send a `BYE` frame, then close. The server flushes
  pending RESPONSE frames first.

### 2.1 Frame format (so you can write a client in any language)

```
16-byte header (big-endian)
+-----------+-----+-------+-------+-------+----------+----------+
|  "DLTA"   | ver | flags | type  | rsvd  |    rid   |    len   |
|   4 B     |  1  |   1   |   1   |   1   |    4 B   |    4 B   |
+-----------+-----+-------+-------+-------+----------+----------+
 then `len` bytes of UTF-8 JSON payload
```

* `magic` is always the four bytes `0x44 0x4C 0x54 0x41` (`"DLTA"`).
* `ver = 1`, `flags = 0` (reserved for future compression).
* `len ≤ 64 MiB`.
* `rid` is client-assigned. The server echoes the same `rid` in the
  matching `RESPONSE` or `ERROR` frame. `PUBLISH` frames use `rid = 0`.

Frame `type` byte:

```
client → server                     server → client
  0x01 HELLO                          0x02 HELLO_OK
  0x03 AUTH                           0x04 AUTH_OK
  0x05 REQUEST                        0x06 RESPONSE
                                      0x07 ERROR
  0x08 PING                           0x09 PONG
  0x0A SUB
  0x0B UNSUB
                                      0x0C PUBLISH
  0x0F BYE
```

Payload schemas (all UTF-8 JSON):

```json
HELLO    { "client": "name/version" }
AUTH     { "username": "admin", "password": "admin" }
         or { "token": "<existing-token>" }     // resume an existing session
REQUEST  { "method":"POST", "path":"/api/v1/...",
           "query": {...}, "headers": {...}, "body": {...} }
RESPONSE { "status": 200, "body": {<envelope>} }
ERROR    { "code": -1, "message": "..." }
SUB      { "channel": "..." }
UNSUB    { "channel": "..." }
PUBLISH  { "channel": "...", "message": "..." }    // server → client only
PING / PONG / BYE   {}
```

### 2.2 Minimal Python client — copy this verbatim

This is a complete, production-shaped client in ~70 lines. Reuse one
`Delta` instance for the whole life of your process — it holds the
single multiplexed socket.

```python
import json, socket, struct, threading
from itertools import count
from queue import Queue, Empty

_HDR = struct.Struct(">4sBBBBII")          # magic ver flg typ rsv rid len
_MAGIC = b"DLTA"

class Delta:
    """One persistent multiplexed connection to deltaql://host:16889.

    Open once, call .auth() once, then call .request() / .subscribe()
    as many times as you like for the lifetime of your process.
    Thread-safe — many threads may call request() concurrently and the
    rid-correlated reader thread routes each response back to its caller.
    """
    # Frame types we care about
    HELLO, HELLO_OK = 0x01, 0x02
    AUTH,  AUTH_OK  = 0x03, 0x04
    REQUEST, RESPONSE, ERROR = 0x05, 0x06, 0x07
    PING, PONG               = 0x08, 0x09
    SUB,  UNSUB,    PUBLISH  = 0x0A, 0x0B, 0x0C
    BYE                      = 0x0F

    def __init__(self, host="127.0.0.1", port=16889, timeout=30):
        self.s = socket.create_connection((host, port), timeout=timeout)
        self.s.setsockopt(socket.IPPROTO_TCP, socket.TCP_NODELAY, 1)
        self.s.setsockopt(socket.SOL_SOCKET, socket.SO_KEEPALIVE, 1)
        self._rid = count(1)
        self._pending: dict = {}              # rid → Queue (response slot)
        self._subs: dict   = {}               # channel → callback
        self._wlock = threading.Lock()
        threading.Thread(target=self._read_loop, daemon=True).start()
        # HELLO is fire-and-forget — server replies with HELLO_OK which
        # the reader simply ignores. No need to block on it.
        self._send(self.HELLO, {"client": "py-llm/1.0"})

    # ---- public API -------------------------------------------------
    def auth(self, username, password):
        # AUTH returns a normal RESPONSE envelope carrying the token.
        env = self._call(self.AUTH, {"username": username, "password": password})
        return env["body"]                                 # → data.token, ...

    def request(self, method, path, body=None, query=None, headers=None):
        payload = {"method": method.upper(), "path": path}
        if body is not None:    payload["body"]    = body
        if query:               payload["query"]   = query
        if headers:             payload["headers"] = headers
        env = self._call(self.REQUEST, payload)
        b = env.get("body", env)
        if isinstance(b, dict) and isinstance(b.get("code"), int) and b["code"] >= 400:
            raise RuntimeError(b.get("message", "delta error"))
        return b["data"] if isinstance(b, dict) and "data" in b else b

    def subscribe(self, channel, callback):
        self._subs[channel] = callback
        self._call(self.SUB, {"channel": channel})

    def unsubscribe(self, channel):
        self._subs.pop(channel, None)
        self._call(self.UNSUB, {"channel": channel})

    def ping(self): self._call(self.PING, {})

    def close(self):
        try: self._send(self.BYE, {})
        except Exception: pass
        self.s.close()

    # ---- wire plumbing ---------------------------------------------
    def _send(self, ftype, payload):
        rid = next(self._rid)
        body = json.dumps(payload).encode()
        with self._wlock:
            self.s.sendall(_HDR.pack(_MAGIC, 1, 0, ftype, 0, rid, len(body)) + body)
        return rid

    def _call(self, ftype, payload, timeout=30):
        rid = next(self._rid)
        q: Queue = Queue(1); self._pending[rid] = q
        body = json.dumps(payload).encode()
        with self._wlock:
            self.s.sendall(_HDR.pack(_MAGIC, 1, 0, ftype, 0, rid, len(body)) + body)
        try:    return q.get(timeout=timeout)
        finally: self._pending.pop(rid, None)

    def _read_loop(self):
        while True:
            try: hdr = self._read_n(16)
            except Exception: return
            if not hdr: return
            magic, _ver, _flg, typ, _rsv, rid, n = _HDR.unpack(hdr)
            if magic != _MAGIC: return
            payload = self._read_n(n) if n else b""
            try: msg = json.loads(payload or b"{}")
            except Exception: msg = {}
            if typ == self.PUBLISH:                # server-push: deliver to callback
                cb = self._subs.get(msg.get("channel"))
                if cb:
                    try: cb(msg.get("channel"), msg.get("message"))
                    except Exception: pass
                continue
            if typ in (self.HELLO_OK, self.AUTH_OK, self.PONG):
                # HELLO_OK / AUTH_OK / PONG are informational. The token
                # is already inside the RESPONSE envelope we returned to
                # auth(). Skipping these prevents stray frames from
                # filling the next caller's slot.
                continue
            q = self._pending.get(rid)
            if q is not None:
                q.put(msg)

    def _read_n(self, k):
        buf = b""
        while len(buf) < k:
            chunk = self.s.recv(k - len(buf))
            if not chunk: return b""
            buf += chunk
        return buf
```

Use it like a database handle — **one instance, many calls**:

```python
db = Delta("127.0.0.1", 16889)
db.auth("admin", "admin")                                     # ← once

# All of these reuse the same socket. Sticky auth means no re-login.
print(db.request("GET",  "/api/v1/health"))
print(db.request("POST", "/api/v1/use", body={"database":"shop","schema":"public"}))

orders = "/api/v1/collections/orders/documents"
db.request("POST", orders, body={"_id":"o1","user_id":"u1","total":12.5})
print(db.request("POST", "/api/v1/collections/orders/documents/search",
                 body={"filter": {"total": {"$gt": 10}}, "limit": 50}))

# Live pubsub — server pushes onto the same socket
db.subscribe("trades", lambda ch, msg: print("push:", ch, msg))
db.request("POST", "/api/v1/pubsub/publish",
           body={"channel":"trades","message":"hello"})

# When the process exits:
db.close()
```

That's it. No connection pool, no re-auth, no HTTP overhead. The
connection sits there as long as your process is alive, multiplexing
every operation including server push.

---

## 3 · Browser / WebSocket version

Browsers can't open raw TCP sockets, so use `ws://<host>:16890/` — same
protocol, JSON-text frames. Open it once at session start, reuse forever.

```js
class Delta {
  constructor(url = "ws://127.0.0.1:16890/") {
    this.url = url;
    this.ws = null;
    this.rid = 0;
    this.pending = new Map();           // rid → {resolve, reject}
    this.subs = new Map();              // channel → callback
  }

  async connect() {
    return new Promise((resolve, reject) => {
      this.ws = new WebSocket(this.url);
      this.ws.onopen = () => {
        this._send({ type: "hello", rid: ++this.rid, client: "js-llm/1.0" });
        resolve();
      };
      this.ws.onmessage = ev => this._onMessage(JSON.parse(ev.data));
      this.ws.onerror = e => reject(e);
      this.ws.onclose = () => { for (const p of this.pending.values()) p.reject(new Error("closed")); };
    });
  }

  // sticky auth — call ONCE, the server keeps the token attached forever.
  async auth(username, password) {
    return this._call({ type: "auth", username, password });
  }

  async request(method, path, body, query, headers) {
    return this._call({ type: "request", method, path,
                        ...(body ? {body} : {}),
                        ...(query ? {query} : {}),
                        ...(headers ? {headers} : {}) });
  }

  // server-push pubsub on the SAME socket — no polling, no second connection.
  async subscribe(channel, callback) {
    this.subs.set(channel, callback);
    return this._call({ type: "subscribe", channel });
  }
  async unsubscribe(channel) {
    this.subs.delete(channel);
    return this._call({ type: "unsubscribe", channel });
  }

  close() { this._send({ type: "bye" }); this.ws.close(); }

  // ---- internals ----
  _send(frame) { this.ws.send(JSON.stringify({ rid: ++this.rid, ...frame })); }
  _call(frame) {
    const rid = ++this.rid;
    return new Promise((resolve, reject) => {
      this.pending.set(rid, { resolve, reject });
      this.ws.send(JSON.stringify({ rid, ...frame }));
    });
  }
  _onMessage(m) {
    if (m.type === "publish") {
      const cb = this.subs.get(m.channel);
      if (cb) cb(m.channel, m.message);
      return;
    }
    const p = this.pending.get(m.rid);
    if (!p) return;
    this.pending.delete(m.rid);
    if (m.type === "error") p.reject(new Error(m.message ?? "delta error"));
    else                    p.resolve(m.body ?? m);
  }
}

// --- usage --------------------------------------------------------
const db = new Delta();
await db.connect();
await db.auth("admin", "admin");                  // ← once

await db.request("POST", "/api/v1/use",
                 { database: "shop", schema: "public" });

await db.request("POST", "/api/v1/collections/orders/documents",
                 { _id: "o1", user_id: "u1", total: 12.5 });

await db.subscribe("trades", (ch, msg) => console.log("push:", ch, msg));
await db.request("POST", "/api/v1/pubsub/publish",
                 { channel: "trades", message: "hello" });
```

The body of every `request(...)` call is **identical** to the body you'd
POST over plain HTTP — same JSON, same field names. Once you know the
endpoint catalog (§5–§14 below), you know both transports.

---

## 4 · Quick HTTP fallback (for one-off scripts only)

For `curl`, migrations, or anywhere a long-lived socket isn't practical.
Each request pays a TCP+HTTP setup cost (≈100–300 µs locally, much more
over the network) and you must attach `Authorization` to every call.

### Login → token

```bash
TOKEN=$(curl -s -X POST http://127.0.0.1:16888/api/v1/auth/login \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{"username":"admin","password":"admin"}' | jq -r .data.token)

curl -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" http://127.0.0.1:16888/api/v1/auth/me
```

### Switch active database/schema for this token

```bash
curl -X POST http://127.0.0.1:16888/api/v1/use \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{"database":"shop","schema":"public"}'
```

After `/use`, all collection routes default to `shop.public.*`. You can
override per request with `?database=...&schema=...` query params or per
body (`{"database":"...","schema":"..."}`).

### How the same call looks across all three transports

In the deltaql:// and ws:// rows below, `db` is the persistent-connection
class you copied from §2.2 (Python) or §3 (browser). It is **not** an
external SDK — it is a few dozen lines of code that lives inside your
own project.

| Transport     | What you send                                                                                            |
| ------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `deltaql://`  | `db.request("POST", "/api/v1/collections/users/documents", body={"_id":"u1","email":"a@b.c"})`           |
| `ws://`       | `await db.request("POST", "/api/v1/collections/users/documents", { _id:"u1", email:"a@b.c" })`           |
| `http://`     | `curl -X POST -H "Authorization: Bearer $T" -d '{"_id":"u1","email":"a@b.c"}' .../api/v1/collections/users/documents` |

Every endpoint below shows the body. **Use the same body inside a
`REQUEST` frame on a persistent connection** — that is always the
preferred form.

---

## 5 · Health / stats / status (no auth)

```bash
GET /api/v1/health      → {"data":{"ok":true}}
GET /api/v1/stats       → server, storage, cache, vector, requests_per_sec, uptime, ...
GET /api/v1/status
GET /api/v1/auth/me     (auth required)
```

---

## 6 · Databases & schemas

```
GET    /api/v1/databases                            list
POST   /api/v1/databases                {"name":"shop","comment":"..."}
GET    /api/v1/databases/{db}
PATCH  /api/v1/databases/{db}           {"comment":"..."}
DELETE /api/v1/databases/{db}
GET    /api/v1/databases/{db}/schemas
POST   /api/v1/databases/{db}/schemas   {"name":"tenant_42"}
DELETE /api/v1/databases/{db}/schemas/{schema}
```

---

## 7 · Collections & indexes

Collections are schemaless documents+vectors namespaces. Only `name` is
required. Optional: `database`, `schema` (= namespace, NOT field types),
`indexes`.

```
GET    /api/v1/collections                          list (in current db)
POST   /api/v1/collections        {
    "name": "orders",
    "indexes": [
      {"name":"by_user",  "fields":["user_id"]},
      {"name":"uniq_ext", "fields":["external_id"], "unique": true}
    ]
  }
GET    /api/v1/collections/{c}
PATCH  /api/v1/collections/{c}                      alter
DELETE /api/v1/collections/{c}
POST   /api/v1/collections/{c}/indexes              {"name":..., "fields":[...], "unique":bool}
DELETE /api/v1/collections/{c}/indexes/{name}
```

---

## 8 · Documents

A document is JSON. `_id` is auto-assigned if you omit it (returned in
`data.id` on insert). On the wire user fields live under `data.data`;
look for your fields under `record.data` and merge them with the
top-level metadata client-side. The Python `Delta` class in §2.2 already
does this for you when you call `db.request(...)` because the server
response envelope is returned as-is — see the example after §2.2 for
the exact shape.

```
POST   /api/v1/collections/{c}/documents              {...doc}
POST   /api/v1/collections/{c}/documents/bulk         {"documents":[...]}
GET    /api/v1/collections/{c}/documents/{id}
PATCH  /api/v1/collections/{c}/documents/{id}         {"$set":{...},"$inc":{...}}    ← MUST use operators
DELETE /api/v1/collections/{c}/documents/{id}
POST   /api/v1/collections/{c}/documents/search       {filter, sort?, limit?, skip?, fields?}
POST   /api/v1/collections/{c}/count                  {filter}
POST   /api/v1/collections/{c}/aggregate              {pipeline:[{$match}, {$group}, ...]}
```

### 8.1 Filter operators (search · count · `$match`)

```
{ "field": value }                          equality
{ "field": { "$eq":  v } }                  ($ne, $gt, $gte, $lt, $lte)
{ "field": { "$in":  [...] } }              ($nin)
{ "field": { "$exists":   true } }
{ "field": { "$regex":    "^a" } }          ECMA regex against stringified value
{ "field": { "$contains": "abc" } }         substring or array-includes
{ "$and": [ {...}, {...} ] }                ($or, $nor, $not)
```

Sort: `{"field": 1}` ascending, `{"field": -1}` descending. Multiple
keys honored in object-insertion order.

### 8.2 Update operators (PATCH body)

```
{ "$set":      { "f": v, "nested.key": v } }
{ "$unset":    { "f": true } }
{ "$inc":      { "score": 1 } }       negative for decrement
{ "$mul":      { "price": 1.1 } }
{ "$push":     { "tags": "x" } }
{ "$pull":     { "tags": "x" } }
{ "$addToSet": { "tags": "x" } }
{ "$rename":   { "old": "new" } }
```

A bare object does **not** replace the document. You must use operators.

### 8.3 Aggregation pipeline stages

```
{ "$match":  { ...filter... } }
{ "$group":  { "_id": "field",
               "out": {"$sum": "src"},
               ...    {"$avg":/$min/$max/$count: "src"} } }
{ "$sort":   { "field": 1 } }
{ "$limit":  N }
{ "$skip":   N }
```

---

## 9 · Vectors (HNSW) — per-collection k-NN

```
POST   /api/v1/collections/{c}/vectors           {"_id":"...", "vector":[...], "metadata":{...}}
DELETE /api/v1/collections/{c}/vectors/{id}
POST   /api/v1/collections/{c}/vectors/search    {"vector":[...], "k":10, "filter":{...}}
                                                 → data: [{_id, score, metadata}, ...]
```

There is no separate index-create step. The first upsert builds the
HNSW graph automatically. Vectors live alongside documents but use
their own storage; a collection may use only documents, only vectors,
or both.

---

## 10 · Cache (Redis-style; persisted)

The cache survives restart when the server runs with `--data DIR`
(default install: `~/.deltaql`).

### KV

```
POST   /api/v1/cache                  {"key":"k","value":"v","ttl":60}     ttl optional, seconds
GET    /api/v1/cache/{key}                                                  → data: {key, value, ttl}
DELETE /api/v1/cache/{key}
POST   /api/v1/cache/{key}/expire     {"ttl":300}
POST   /api/v1/cache/{key}/incr       {"delta":1}                           → data: {value: <int>}
```

### Hash

```
POST   /api/v1/cache/{key}/hash       {"field":"name","value":"alice"}
GET    /api/v1/cache/{key}/hash                                            all fields
GET    /api/v1/cache/{key}/hash/{f}
DELETE /api/v1/cache/{key}/hash/{f}
```

### List (push **one** value per call; choose direction)

```
POST   /api/v1/cache/{key}/list/push  {"value":"x","direction":"left"|"right"}   → data: {length}
GET    /api/v1/cache/{key}/list?start=0&stop=-1                                  → data: {items:[...]}
```

### Set

```
POST   /api/v1/cache/{key}/set        {"member":"alice"}                   → data: {added:0|1}
GET    /api/v1/cache/{key}/set                                             → data: {members:[...]}
DELETE /api/v1/cache/{key}/set/{member}
```

### Sorted set

```
POST   /api/v1/cache/{key}/zset       {"member":"alice","score":42.0}
GET    /api/v1/cache/{key}/zset?start=0&stop=-1                            → data: {items:[{member,score}]}
```

---

## 11 · Pub/Sub

Anyone with permission can publish:

```
POST /api/v1/pubsub/publish    {"channel":"trades","message":"<string>"}
                                → data: {subscribers: N}
```

To **receive** pushes you need a persistent transport. **Don't poll.**

* On `deltaql://`: send a `SUB` frame with `{"channel":"..."}`. The
  server delivers `PUBLISH` frames (type=0x0C, rid=0) on the same
  socket whenever anyone publishes. Use the Python client in §2.2:
  `db.subscribe("trades", lambda ch, msg: ...)`.
* On `ws://`: send `{"type":"subscribe","channel":"..."}`. Inbound
  `{"type":"publish","channel":"...","message":"..."}` frames arrive
  on the same WebSocket. Use the JS client in §3:
  `await db.subscribe("trades", (ch, msg) => ...)`.
* On `http://`: not possible. Switch transports.

You can **publish** over any transport, including HTTP. Subscribing
requires persistent.

---

## 12 · Users · Roles · Permissions (RBAC)

Privileges are case-insensitive strings:
`SELECT INSERT UPDATE DELETE CREATE DROP ALTER INDEX TRUNCATE REFERENCES TRIGGER EXECUTE USAGE CONNECT`.

Built-in roles: `superuser, db_admin, user_admin, read_write, read_only, public`.

```
GET    /api/v1/users
POST   /api/v1/users                {"username":"alice","password":"s3cret",
                                     "default_database":"shop","default_schema":"public"}
GET    /api/v1/users/{u}
PATCH  /api/v1/users/{u}            {"password":"new"}
DELETE /api/v1/users/{u}
POST   /api/v1/users/{u}/lock
POST   /api/v1/users/{u}/unlock
POST   /api/v1/users/{u}/roles      {"role":"read_write"}
DELETE /api/v1/users/{u}/roles/{role}

GET    /api/v1/roles
POST   /api/v1/roles                {"name":"auditor","description":"..."}
DELETE /api/v1/roles/{name}

GET    /api/v1/permissions
POST   /api/v1/permissions/grant      {"role":"auditor","privileges":["SELECT"],
                                       "target":{"type":"collection","database":"shop",
                                                 "schema":"public","collection":"orders"}}
POST   /api/v1/permissions/grant-all  {"role":"auditor",
                                       "target":{"type":"database","database":"shop"}}
POST   /api/v1/permissions/revoke     {role, privileges, target}
```

### Row-level security (RLS)

```
POST /api/v1/databases/{db}/schemas/{s}/collections/{c}/rls
     {"rules":[{"role":"auditor","predicate":{"tenant_id":42}}]}
GET  /api/v1/databases/{db}/schemas/{s}/collections/{c}/policies
POST /api/v1/databases/{db}/schemas/{s}/collections/{c}/policies
DELETE /api/v1/databases/{db}/schemas/{s}/collections/{c}/policies/{p}
```

---

## 13 · Errors

| `code` | HTTP | Meaning                                         |
| ------ | ---- | ----------------------------------------------- |
| 0      | 200  | OK                                              |
| 1      | 500  | Generic error                                   |
| 2      | 404  | NotFound — collection / doc / user / role       |
| 3      | 409  | Duplicate — `_id` exists, role exists, ...      |
| 4      | 400  | Invalid — bad field, validation, ...            |
| 5      | 401  | Unauthorized — missing/expired token            |
| 6      | 403  | Forbidden — RBAC denied / read-only replica     |
| 7      | 500  | Internal                                        |

Replicas reject mutations with HTTP 403 + body
`{"role":"replica","master_url":"http://master:16888"}` — retry against
the master URL.

Over `deltaql://`, the server sends a frame of type `ERROR (0x07)` with
`{"code": -1, "message": "..."}` for protocol-level failures. For
endpoint-level errors (NotFound, Forbidden, etc.) it sends a normal
`RESPONSE` frame whose `body.code` carries the Status enum — handle it
the same way you would an HTTP error response.

---

## 14 · Replication / cluster

Delta supports two replication modes; pick exactly one per cluster.

### 14.1 · Legacy master/replica streaming (single writer)

```
GET  /api/v1/cluster/info                role, lsn, master_url, replicas[]
GET  /api/v1/cluster/changes?since=N     WAL changes since LSN
GET  /api/v1/cluster/snapshot            full bootstrap snapshot
POST /api/v1/cluster/promote             this replica → master
POST /api/v1/cluster/demote              {"master_url":"http://..."}
```

Boot a replica:

```bash
delta_server --role replica --master-url http://master:16888 --cluster-token <secret> ...
```

Failover is manual: a human (or an external orchestrator) hits
`/cluster/promote` on the chosen replica.

### 14.2 · Raft consensus (automatic failover)

When `--enable-raft` is set on every node, every `put / del` on the
leader is synchronously replicated through Raft; an HTTP `200` from a
mutating endpoint means the write is on a majority AND visible locally.
Followers reject writes with `503 raft: not leader` so clients are
expected to retry against the current leader (see
`GET /api/v1/cluster/raft/status → data.leader_id`).

Boot a three-node cluster (one shell per node, swap `--node-id`):

```bash
delta_server \
  --enable-raft --node-id n1 \
  --cluster-peer n1@host1:16888 \
  --cluster-peer n2@host2:16888 \
  --cluster-peer n3@host3:16888 \
  --cluster-token <secret> \
  --port 16888 --data /var/lib/delta/n1
```

Tunables (CLI or JSON):

```
--raft-election-min-ms       (default 200)
--raft-election-max-ms       (default 400)
--raft-heartbeat-ms          (default 50)
--raft-tick-ms               (default 20)
--raft-snapshot-threshold N  (default 10000 committed entries; 0 = off)
--raft-propose-timeout-ms N  (default 5000)
--raft-no-pre-vote           (disable §9.6 pre-vote; debug only)
```

Endpoints:

```
POST /api/v1/cluster/raft/vote               (peer-to-peer, token-gated)
POST /api/v1/cluster/raft/append             (peer-to-peer, token-gated)
POST /api/v1/cluster/raft/install_snapshot   (peer-to-peer, token-gated)
GET  /api/v1/cluster/raft/status             role, term, leader_id, peers[], commit_index
POST /api/v1/cluster/raft/propose            opaque payload — for tests
POST /api/v1/cluster/raft/snapshot           force a snapshot now (superuser)
POST /api/v1/cluster/raft/add_peer           { "peer_id": "n4" }     (superuser)
POST /api/v1/cluster/raft/remove_peer        { "peer_id": "n3" }     (superuser)
```

Membership uses the simpler single-server change (Raft thesis §4.3) —
one add or remove at a time, the new peer set takes effect on append,
and the leader refuses overlapping changes.

### 14.3 · Sharding (horizontal scale-out)

Each `delta_server` process belongs to exactly ONE shard. Every node
runs a consistent-hash gateway that knows the full shard map and
routes document operations to the owning shard. Sharding and Raft
compose: inside one shard, run raft for replication; across shards,
the gateway hashes by document id.

Boot two shards × one node (one shell per node, swap `--shard-id`):

```bash
delta_server \
  --enable-sharding --shard-id s0 \
  --shard 's0=n1@host1:16888' \
  --shard 's1=n2@host2:16888' \
  --cluster-token <secret> \
  --port 16888 --data /var/lib/delta/n1
```

For a fault-tolerant deployment, each shard SHOULD be a 3-node Raft
group (combine `--enable-raft` + `--enable-sharding` on the same
process; the gateway routes between shards, Raft replicates within a
shard).

Tunables:

```
--shard-vnodes N          virtual nodes per shard (default 256, min 128)
--shard-rpc-timeout-ms N  per-inter-shard call deadline (default 2500)
```

Endpoints:

```
GET  /api/v1/cluster/shards                     introspection
GET  /api/v1/cluster/shards/route?key=<id>      where does <id> live?
```

Routing semantics:

  * **Point ops** (`POST /collections/{c}/documents`,
    `GET|PATCH|DELETE /collections/{c}/documents/{id}`,
    `POST /collections/{c}/documents/bulk`) — gateway computes
    `route(_id)` and forwards to the owning shard's first peer. Doc
    ids are allocated by the gateway when missing so routing is stable
    from the first write.
  * **Fan-out reads** (`POST /collections/{c}/documents/search`,
    `POST /collections/{c}/count`, `POST /collections/{c}/aggregate`) —
    gateway scatters to every shard in parallel and concatenates +
    re-applies skip/limit on the merged result. NOTE: pipelines that
    rely on `$group` or `$sort` for global correctness should keep
    their data on a single shard.
  * **Cross-shard transactions** (`POST /transactions/execute`) — if
    ops span multiple shards the gateway returns HTTP 501 with
    `{"code": 501, "message": "cross-shard transactions are not
    supported in this release", "data": {"shards": [...]}}`. Single-
    shard transactions transparently forward.
  * **Collection metadata** (`POST /api/v1/collections`) is
    automatically broadcast to every peer shard so each shard holds
    a full copy of the schema. Create once on any gateway.

Re-balancing after a topology change: run
`tools/delta-reshard --gateway <url> --shard <url1> --shard <url2>`.
It walks every collection, recomputes ownership under the new
topology, and moves docs that landed on the wrong shard.

### 14.4 · Multi-master writes (active-active)

Per-collection opt-in. When a collection is created with
`{"multi_master": true}`, every write to it gets stamped with a
**hybrid logical clock** (`_hlc` field, 16-hex-char lexicographic) and
deletes become tombstones instead of physical removes. A background
puller pulls changes from each configured peer and merges them under
last-writer-wins keyed on `_hlc`.

Boot two active-active nodes (each writing to BOTH from real clients):

```bash
# n1
delta_server --port 16888 --data /var/lib/delta/n1 \
  --mm-peer http://n2:16888 \
  --mm-poll-ms 500 \
  --cluster-token <secret>

# n2
delta_server --port 16888 --data /var/lib/delta/n2 \
  --mm-peer http://n1:16888 \
  --mm-poll-ms 500 \
  --cluster-token <secret>
```

Create the collection with the flag set on each node (no auto-broadcast
in this orthogonal-to-sharding mode):

```http
POST /api/v1/collections
Authorization: Bearer <tok>
Content-Type: application/json

{"name": "items", "multi_master": true}
```

After that, `POST /collections/items/documents` on either node
converges to both within `--mm-poll-ms`. Concurrent writes to the same
`_id` on different nodes resolve to the higher `_hlc`. Concurrent
delete + update races resolve the same way.

Endpoints:

```
POST /api/v1/cluster/mm/pull   (cluster-token gated)
                               body: { "database", "schema",
                                       "collection", "since_hlc",
                                       "limit" }
                               reply: { "changes": [...], "max_hlc" }
POST /api/v1/cluster/mm/push   apply remote changes; reply
                               { "applied", "skipped", "failed" }
GET  /api/v1/cluster/mm/status authenticated; per-peer cursors and
                               counters for monitoring
```

Caveats:

  * Multi-master is **last-writer-wins per document**. There is no
    field-level merge (no OR-set, no LWW-register-per-field). Apps
    that need additive semantics should encode that themselves
    (e.g. server-side `$inc` against a single-master collection).
  * Tombstones are kept forever in this release; a future GC pass
    will compact them once every peer has acknowledged.
  * Multi-master is independent of sharding. Combining the two
    means each shard runs its own active-active pair — workable
    but not auto-configured by tooling yet.

---

## 15 · Complete REST endpoint index

```
auth/session
  POST   /api/v1/auth/login                           { username, password } → { token, ... }
  POST   /api/v1/auth/logout
  GET    /api/v1/auth/me
  POST   /api/v1/use                                  { database, schema }

introspection
  GET    /api/v1/health                               (no auth)
  GET    /api/v1/stats
  GET    /api/v1/status

databases / schemas
  GET    /api/v1/databases
  POST   /api/v1/databases                            { name, ... }
  GET    /api/v1/databases/{db}
  PATCH  /api/v1/databases/{db}
  DELETE /api/v1/databases/{db}
  GET    /api/v1/databases/{db}/schemas
  POST   /api/v1/databases/{db}/schemas
  DELETE /api/v1/databases/{db}/schemas/{schema}
  GET    /api/v1/databases/{db}/schemas/{s}/collections/{c}/policies
  POST   /api/v1/databases/{db}/schemas/{s}/collections/{c}/policies
  DELETE /api/v1/databases/{db}/schemas/{s}/collections/{c}/policies/{p}
  POST   /api/v1/databases/{db}/schemas/{s}/collections/{c}/rls

collections / documents
  GET    /api/v1/collections                          ?database=
  POST   /api/v1/collections                          { name, indexes?, database?, schema? }
  GET    /api/v1/collections/{c}
  PATCH  /api/v1/collections/{c}
  DELETE /api/v1/collections/{c}
  POST   /api/v1/collections/{c}/indexes              { name, fields, unique? }
  DELETE /api/v1/collections/{c}/indexes/{name}
  POST   /api/v1/collections/{c}/documents            { ...doc }
  POST   /api/v1/collections/{c}/documents/bulk       { documents:[...] }
  GET    /api/v1/collections/{c}/documents/{id}
  PATCH  /api/v1/collections/{c}/documents/{id}       { $set/$inc/... }
  DELETE /api/v1/collections/{c}/documents/{id}
  POST   /api/v1/collections/{c}/documents/search     { filter, sort?, limit?, skip?, fields? }
  POST   /api/v1/collections/{c}/count                { filter }
  POST   /api/v1/collections/{c}/aggregate            { pipeline }

vectors
  POST   /api/v1/collections/{c}/vectors              { _id, vector, metadata? }
  DELETE /api/v1/collections/{c}/vectors/{id}
  POST   /api/v1/collections/{c}/vectors/search       { vector, k, filter? }

cache
  POST   /api/v1/cache                                { key, value, ttl? }
  GET    /api/v1/cache/{key}
  DELETE /api/v1/cache/{key}
  POST   /api/v1/cache/{key}/expire                   { ttl }
  POST   /api/v1/cache/{key}/incr                     { delta }
  POST   /api/v1/cache/{key}/hash                     { field, value }
  GET    /api/v1/cache/{key}/hash
  GET    /api/v1/cache/{key}/hash/{field}
  DELETE /api/v1/cache/{key}/hash/{field}
  POST   /api/v1/cache/{key}/list/push                { value, direction:"left"|"right" }
  GET    /api/v1/cache/{key}/list                     ?start=&stop=
  POST   /api/v1/cache/{key}/set                      { member }
  GET    /api/v1/cache/{key}/set
  DELETE /api/v1/cache/{key}/set/{member}
  POST   /api/v1/cache/{key}/zset                     { member, score }
  GET    /api/v1/cache/{key}/zset                     ?start=&stop=

pubsub
  POST   /api/v1/pubsub/publish                       { channel, message }
   (subscribe via deltaql:// SUB frame or ws:// {type:"subscribe"} — see §11)

rbac
  GET    /api/v1/users
  POST   /api/v1/users                                { username, password, ... }
  GET    /api/v1/users/{u}
  PATCH  /api/v1/users/{u}
  DELETE /api/v1/users/{u}
  POST   /api/v1/users/{u}/lock
  POST   /api/v1/users/{u}/unlock
  POST   /api/v1/users/{u}/roles                      { role }
  DELETE /api/v1/users/{u}/roles/{role}
  GET    /api/v1/roles
  POST   /api/v1/roles                                { name, description? }
  DELETE /api/v1/roles/{r}
  GET    /api/v1/permissions
  POST   /api/v1/permissions/grant                    { role, privileges:[...], target }
  POST   /api/v1/permissions/grant-all                { role, target }
  POST   /api/v1/permissions/revoke                   { role, privileges:[...], target }

cluster
  GET    /api/v1/cluster/info
  GET    /api/v1/cluster/changes                      ?since=
  GET    /api/v1/cluster/snapshot
  POST   /api/v1/cluster/promote
  POST   /api/v1/cluster/demote                       { master_url }

ops
  GET    /api/v1/connections
  DELETE /api/v1/connections/{id}
  GET    /llms.txt                                    (this file; no auth)
```

Every mutating route honors `Authorization: Bearer <token>` over HTTP, or
the sticky AUTH state of a `deltaql://` / `ws://` connection.

---

## 16 · Recipes

### 16.1 Long-running agent: ONE persistent connection for everything

```python
from delta_persistent import Delta     # the class from §2.2

db = Delta()                                              # opens once
db.auth("admin", "admin")                                 # logs in once
db.request("POST", "/api/v1/use", body={"database":"shop","schema":"public"})

def insert_order(o):
    db.request("POST", "/api/v1/collections/orders/documents", body=o)

def search_orders(uid):
    return db.request("POST",
        "/api/v1/collections/orders/documents/search",
        body={"filter":{"user_id":uid}, "limit":50})

# Live event tap on the SAME socket — no second connection needed:
db.subscribe("orders.events", lambda ch, msg: print("event:", msg))

while True:                                                # business logic loop
    insert_order(...)
    print(search_orders(...))
```

The connection lives as long as the process. No connection pooling, no
re-auth, no HTTP overhead.

### 16.2 Cache-aside read-through

```python
def get_user(uid):
    cached = db.request("GET", f"/api/v1/cache/user:{uid}").get("value")
    if cached is not None:
        return json.loads(cached)
    raw = db.request("GET", f"/api/v1/collections/users/documents/{uid}")
    user = raw["data"]                                  # flatten if you want
    db.request("POST", "/api/v1/cache",
               body={"key":f"user:{uid}","value":json.dumps(user),"ttl":300})
    return user
```

### 16.3 Vector RAG ingest + query

```python
for chunk in chunks:
    db.request("POST", "/api/v1/collections/docs/vectors",
               body={"_id": chunk.id, "vector": chunk.embedding,
                     "metadata":{"src": chunk.src}})

hits = db.request("POST", "/api/v1/collections/docs/vectors/search",
                  body={"vector": query_embedding, "k": 10,
                        "filter": {"src": "manual"}})
```

### 16.4 Pubsub fan-out

```python
# Producer (any process, any transport — even one-shot HTTP curl)
db.request("POST", "/api/v1/pubsub/publish",
           body={"channel":"trades","message": json.dumps({"side":"buy","qty":42})})

# Consumer (long-running, one persistent connection)
db.subscribe("trades", lambda ch, msg: handle(json.loads(msg)))
# ... never returns — the daemon thread reading the socket invokes the callback.
```

### 16.5 Bootstrap a tenant

```python
db.request("POST", "/api/v1/databases", body={"name":"shop"})
db.request("POST", "/api/v1/databases/shop/schemas", body={"name":"public"})
db.request("POST", "/api/v1/use", body={"database":"shop","schema":"public"})
db.request("POST", "/api/v1/collections",
    body={"name":"orders",
          "indexes":[{"name":"by_user","fields":["user_id"]}]})
db.request("POST", "/api/v1/collections/orders/documents/bulk",
    body={"documents":[{"user_id":"u1","total":12.5},
                       {"user_id":"u2","total":99}]})
```

---

## 17 · Tips for LLM coding agents

1. **Default to a persistent connection.** Open ONE `deltaql://` (Python,
   backend) or `ws://` (browser, edge) connection at process start, AUTH
   once, and reuse it for every subsequent operation. Treat it like a
   MySQL connection handle.
2. **Don't re-open per request.** That's anti-pattern. The server sets
   TCP_NODELAY + KEEPALIVE; one socket can carry millions of requests.
3. **Don't re-authenticate per request.** AUTH is sticky — the server
   keeps your token attached to the connection. After AUTH, REQUEST
   frames need no `Authorization` header.
4. **Use `rid` to multiplex.** Many requests can be in flight at once.
   Match RESPONSE frames back to your callers by the echoed `rid`.
5. **Subscribe on the same socket** for live updates. No separate
   connection. No polling. The server pushes PUBLISH frames whenever
   anyone publishes.
6. **HTTP is the fallback.** Use it for one-off `curl` commands and
   migration scripts. Don't build a long-running app on top of
   HTTP-per-request.
7. **Always check `code` in the response body**, not just the HTTP
   status — `code != 0/200` always means failure.
8. **`_id` is optional** on insert. The server assigns a snowflake-ish
   ID returned in `data.id`.
9. **Filters use Mongo-ish `$` operators**, not SQL. (§8.1)
10. **Updates require operators** (`$set`, `$inc`, ...). A bare object
    does NOT replace the document. (§8.2)
11. **List endpoints wrap items** under a key — `data.users`,
    `data.documents`, `data.items`, `data.members`, etc.
12. **Documents wrap user fields** under `data.data` on the wire. To
    get a flat object, merge the top-level metadata with `data.data`
    client-side. The §2.2 client returns the raw envelope so you can
    do this in your own helper.
13. **Field-name gotchas:** `incr` uses `delta` (not `by`); `list/push`
    uses `value` (not `values`); `set` uses `member` (not `members`).
14. **The cache survives restart** when the server has `--data DIR`.
15. **Replicas reject mutations** with HTTP 403; the body's
    `master_url` tells you where to retry.
16. **Frame size cap is 64 MiB.** Use `documents/bulk` or chunk uploads
    for huge writes.
17. **Pagination is `limit + skip`.** No cursor API. Sort by `_id` or
    `created_at` for stable ordering across pages.
18. **CORS is open** (`Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *`), so browsers can
    call HTTP and WebSocket directly.
19. **`/llms.txt` is your friend.** Re-fetch it whenever you doubt an
    endpoint shape.

---

## 18 · Where the source lives

If anything below disagrees with the live server, the source wins —
file a bug at <https://github.com/ProtectsAPP/delta>.

* Persistent protocol server (this is the MySQL-equivalent):
  `src/network/deltaql_server.hpp`, `src/network/deltaql_protocol.hpp`
* WebSocket bridge (the same protocol over JSON for browsers):
  `src/network/ws_server.hpp`
* HTTP route table & request shapes:
  `src/network/http_server.hpp`
* Filter / update operators:
  `src/core/document.hpp`
* Aggregation pipeline:
  `src/core/collection.hpp`
* Auth & RBAC:
  `src/auth/auth_manager.hpp`
* End-to-end protocol smoke test (run all three transports):
  `scripts/test_protocols.py`

**Everything in this file is self-contained.** The §2.2 Python class
and the §3 JavaScript class are *the* clients — copy them straight into
your project. There is nothing to `pip install` and nothing to
`npm install`. If you can open a TCP socket (or a WebSocket) and parse
JSON, you can drive Delta in any language with the frame layout in §2.1.
